text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE
Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Review and New Patient Consult

---

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Medical Oncology
CONSULTING SERVICES: Hepatobiliary Surgery, Colorectal Surgery, Interventional Radiology
REFERRING PHYSICIAN: Gastroenterology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Okafor is a 58-year-old Nigerian-American male, high school principal, who presents for formal medical oncology new patient consultation and multidisciplinary tumor board review following recent diagnosis of stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases. He was referred by gastroenterology following colonoscopy and subsequent staging workup.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Okafor initially presented to his primary care physician approximately six weeks ago with a several-month history of intermittent hematochezia, a change in bowel habits (increasingly narrow caliber stools), and approximately 8-pound unintentional weight loss over four months. He had no prior history of colorectal polyps and had not undergone prior colorectal cancer screening (he reports difficulty scheduling colonoscopy due to work demands). He was referred urgently for colonoscopy.

Colonosopy revealed a large, partially obstructing, ulcerated mass in the sigmoid colon at approximately 30 cm from the anal verge. The mass was biopsied and a separate 3 cm sessile polyp was noted adjacent to the primary lesion. Staging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrated the 4.8 cm sigmoid colon mass with associated pericolic fat stranding and multiple enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes (largest 1.9 cm), as well as multiple hypodense hepatic lesions in both lobes -- specifically: segment II (1.4 cm), segment IVa (2.1 cm), segment VI (1.7 cm), and segment VII (3.2 cm, dominant). No pulmonary nodules, no ascites, and no peritoneal implants were identified on preoperative CT imaging.

Pathology from colonoscopic biopsy of the sigmoid mass confirmed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma (grade 2). Immunohistochemistry showed intact MMR protein expression (MLH1+, MSH2+, MSH6+, PMS2+), consistent with microsatellite stable (MSS) disease. KRAS codon 12 mutation testing returned a G12D mutation (c.35G>A). NRAS exons 2-4 were wild-type. BRAF V600E was wild-type. Concurrent CT-guided core needle biopsy of the hepatic segment VII lesion confirmed metastatic adenocarcinoma consistent with colorectal primary (CK20+, CDX2+, CK7-).

Comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) via a 324-gene panel was performed on the hepatic core biopsy. In addition to the KRAS G12D mutation, the following alterations were identified: TP53 p.R248W (pathogenic, loss-of-function), APC p.R1450* (pathogenic, loss-of-function), SMAD4 p.R361H (pathogenic, associated with poor prognosis and anti-VEGF resistance), PIK3CA p.E545K (pathogenic, activating co-driver), FBXW7 p.R465H (likely pathogenic), CDK8 amplification (4 copies), MYC amplification (6 copies), and PTEN homozygous deletion (exons 1-9). Tumor mutational burden was 8 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status by NGS: MSS. HER2 not amplified. No ERBB2 amplification, no ALK/RET/NTRK fusions. Germline reflex testing was negative for pathogenic variants in APC, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, MUTYH, BRCA1, and BRCA2.

CEA at diagnosis: 187.3 ng/mL. CA 19-9 at diagnosis: 412 U/mL.

Final staging: Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition) sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, pT3 N2a M1a (bilobar hepatic metastases, synchronous).

The patient presents today for tumor board review and formal treatment planning. He is accompanied by his wife. He understands his diagnosis is serious, has been researching his condition, and states he is "ready to fight this." He expresses a strong desire for aggressive therapy and is asking about all available options.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- well-controlled on metformin; most recent HbA1c approximately 7.1% (per PCP records); no history of diabetic ketoacidosis or hyperglycemic emergencies; mild baseline peripheral neuropathy (grade 1) in bilateral feet, attributed to diabetic neuropathy, pre-existing prior to cancer diagnosis.
2. Hypertension -- on lisinopril 10 mg daily at baseline; blood pressure generally well-controlled per PCP notes.
3. Hyperlipidemia -- on atorvastatin; most recent LDL approximately 88 mg/dL.
4. Former smoker -- quit approximately 12 years ago; 15 pack-year history (approximately 1 PPD for 15 years); no known COPD or emphysema on imaging; no current pulmonary symptoms at rest.
5. No prior history of colorectal polyps or colorectal cancer screening.
6. No prior surgeries.
7. No prior radiation therapy.
8. No personal history of other malignancies.

---

FAMILY HISTORY:
- No family history of colorectal cancer.
- Father: hypertension, type 2 diabetes, died of cardiovascular disease at age 71.
- Mother: alive, age 82, hypertension.
- One sibling (brother, age 55): hypertension.
- No family history of Lynch syndrome-associated tumors (endometrial, ovarian, urothelial, gastric cancers). Germline testing in our patient returned negative, consistent with sporadic colorectal cancer.
- No family history of breast or ovarian cancer.
- No family history of pancreatic cancer.

---

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Occupation: High school principal; a demanding job with significant cognitive and administrative responsibilities.
- Marital status: Married; wife present at today's visit; strong social support reported.
- Children: Two adult children.
- Ethnic background: Nigerian-American.
- Tobacco: Former smoker, quit 12 years ago; 15 pack-year history.
- Alcohol: Social use, approximately 2-4 drinks per week; no heavy alcohol use or dependence.
- Illicit drugs: Denied.
- Exercise: Previously moderately active (walking, occasional jogging), now somewhat limited due to fatigue and recent illness.
- Diet: Mixed diet; wife assists with dietary modifications since diagnosis.
- Living situation: Lives with wife in own home.
- Insurance: Covered through employer plan; case manager engaged for prior authorizations.

---

ALLERGIES:
- No known drug allergies.
- No known food or environmental allergies.

---

MEDICATIONS (at time of consultation):
1. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (type 2 diabetes)
2. Lisinopril 10 mg orally daily (hypertension)
3. Atorvastatin 40 mg orally nightly (hyperlipidemia)
4. Aspirin 81 mg orally daily (cardiovascular prophylaxis -- plan to hold perioperatively)
5. Multivitamin daily

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Positive for approximately 8-pound unintentional weight loss over four months. Positive for moderate fatigue (grade 1-2 per CTCAE). Denies fever or chills.
Gastrointestinal: Positive for intermittent hematochezia (present at colonoscopy, ongoing but decreased in frequency). Positive for change in bowel habits with narrower caliber stools. Denies obstipation, abdominal distension suggesting obstruction, nausea, vomiting, or melena. No significant abdominal pain at rest. Mild right upper quadrant discomfort on palpation (possible hepatic capsular distension from metastatic involvement).
Respiratory: Denies dyspnea, cough, hemoptysis. No pulmonary symptoms currently. No pulmonary lesions on staging CT.
Neurological: Positive for grade 1 baseline sensory neuropathy (tingling/numbness in bilateral feet) attributed to pre-existing diabetic peripheral neuropathy. This is clinically significant as it will lower the threshold for oxaliplatin-induced neurotoxicity with planned treatment.
Musculoskeletal: Denies bone pain, joint pain, or pathologic fractures. No bone lesions identified on staging CT.
Cardiovascular: Blood pressure at home reported as 128-136/82-88 mmHg on current lisinopril. Denies chest pain or palpitations. No history of congestive heart failure or prior myocardial infarction.
Urinary: Denies hematuria or changes in urinary frequency.
Endocrine/metabolic: Blood glucose generally well-controlled on metformin; no recent hypoglycemic events.
Psychological: Anxiety related to new diagnosis. Reports good spousal and family support. Expresses strong desire for treatment and maintained hope.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: Well-appearing, well-nourished Nigerian-American male in no acute distress. Appears stated age. Alert and oriented x3. Accompanied by wife.
Vitals: Blood pressure 132/84 mmHg, heart rate 74 beats per minute, respiratory rate 16 per minute, temperature 98.4 degrees F, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Weight: 87.3 kg. Height: 175 cm. BSA approximately 2.01 m2. BMI 28.4 kg/m2.
ECOG Performance Status: 1 (restricted in physically strenuous activity but ambulatory and able to carry out work of a light or sedentary nature).
Head and Neck: No scleral icterus. Mucous membranes moist. No lymphadenopathy in cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary regions. No temporal wasting.
Chest/Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles. Normal respiratory effort.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild tenderness to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant; no voluntary guarding. No peritoneal signs. No palpable abdominal mass. Liver edge palpable approximately 1-2 cm below right costal margin on deep inspiration, consistent with hepatomegaly from metastatic involvement. Spleen not palpable. Bowel sounds normoactive.
Extremities: No lower extremity edema. Decreased sensation to light touch in bilateral feet distally (consistent with pre-existing diabetic neuropathy, grade 1).
Neurological: Alert, oriented, appropriate. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Strength 5/5 throughout. Gait normal.
Skin: No jaundice, palmar erythema, spider angiomata, or caput medusae (no signs of hepatic dysfunction).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
CBC: WBC 6.8 x10^9/L, hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, consistent with anemia of chronic disease/malignancy), platelets 312 x10^9/L.
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, creatinine 0.92 mg/dL (eGFR >60, adequate renal function for planned chemotherapy), total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, AST 38 U/L, ALT 44 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 112 U/L (mildly elevated, consistent with hepatic metastatic burden), albumin 3.7 g/dL.
Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1, within normal limits.
Tumor markers: CEA 187.3 ng/mL (markedly elevated), CA 19-9 412 U/mL (elevated).
HbA1c (recent, per PCP): 7.1%.
Lipids (recent, per PCP): LDL 88 mg/dL, HDL 48 mg/dL, triglycerides 142 mg/dL.

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
Staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast: 4.8 cm sigmoid colon mass with pericolic fat stranding and multiple enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes (largest 1.9 cm). Multiple bilobar hepatic lesions: segment II 1.4 cm, segment IVa 2.1 cm, segment VI 1.7 cm, segment VII 3.2 cm (dominant). No ascites, no peritoneal implants, no pulmonary nodules or masses, no bone lesions.

The imaging was reviewed at multidisciplinary tumor board. Hepatobiliary surgery noted that the bilobar distribution of hepatic metastases -- particularly the segment VII lesion and its proximity to major hepatic veins, and the segment IVa lesion's relationship to the middle hepatic vein -- renders upfront R0 hepatic resection of all lesions technically challenging and oncologically inadvisable without significant tumor downsizing. The segment VI lesion may be amenable to ablative therapy. Primary tumor in sigmoid colon carries a risk of obstruction given its size and partial occlusion.

---

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION:
This case was presented and discussed at the gastrointestinal multidisciplinary tumor board today, with active participation from medical oncology, hepatobiliary surgery, colorectal surgery, and interventional radiology.

Key genomic and molecular considerations:
- KRAS G12D mutation: This is the dominant driver mutation and, critically, renders the patient ineligible for anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab). Any RAS-mutant colorectal cancer -- whether KRAS or NRAS -- is a categorical contraindication to EGFR-targeted antibody therapy, and the G12D variant is among the most common and well-characterized.
- MSS/pMMR status: The tumor is microsatellite stable by both IHC and confirmed by NGS. This precludes use of immune checkpoint inhibitors such as pembrolizumab (Keytruda), which are approved and effective in MSI-H/dMMR colorectal cancer but offer no benefit in MSS disease.
- SMAD4 p.R361H loss-of-function mutation: This is an important prognostic finding. SMAD4 loss in metastatic colorectal cancer has been associated with worse outcomes, resistance to 5-FU-based regimens, and some data suggest association with bevacizumab resistance -- though bevacizumab remains a reasonable option in the first-line setting.
- PIK3CA E545K activating mutation with concurrent PTEN homozygous deletion: Dual PI3K/AKT pathway activation. This may have biologic implications for resistance to therapy and could be a future therapeutic consideration.
- HER2 status: Not amplified on baseline testing. ERBB2 non-amplified, HER2 IHC not performed at this time given lack of amplification by CGP. However, it is noteworthy that HER2 amplification can emerge as an acquired resistance mechanism in colorectal cancer, particularly under selection pressure from anti-EGFR or anti-VEGF therapy; this will warrant re-evaluation at progression.
- KRAS G12D-specific targeted therapies: Investigational agents such as KRAS G12D-selective inhibitors are in early-phase clinical development. At this time, these agents do not have regulatory approval for colorectal cancer and are not available as standard of care. We will track clinical trial availability.
- Germline testing: Negative for Lynch syndrome and FAP-associated mutations; this is a sporadic colorectal cancer.

Treatment approach decided at tumor board:
Given the bilobar hepatic metastatic burden, the technical challenges to upfront hepatic resection, and the molecular profile (KRAS-mutant, MSS), the tumor board consensus recommendation is initiation of palliative-intent systemic chemotherapy with FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab as first-line therapy. This regimen (oxaliplatin/leucovorin/5-FU with anti-VEGF) is well-established as a first-line backbone in RAS-mutant metastatic colorectal cancer. Anti-EGFR therapy is not indicated given KRAS G12D.

The goals of first-line therapy are dual: (1) systemic disease control and potential tumor downsizing, with the secondary aim of converting hepatic metastases to a resectable or ablatable state (conversion strategy); and (2) palliation of symptoms and maintenance of performance status. If hepatic lesions respond sufficiently after 6-8 cycles, the case will be reassessed at MDT for hepatic-directed therapy -- this could include hepatic resection (if all lesions become amenable to R0 resection with adequate FLR), combination resection and intraoperative ablation, or percutaneous ablation of selected lesions (particularly the segment VI lesion noted by interventional radiology). The sigmoid primary will be addressed on a case-by-case basis; currently, the patient does not have evidence of obstruction and deferral of primary resection to proceed with systemic therapy first is appropriate and supported by published data.

Port placement: Subcutaneous implanted venous access device (port) will be placed prior to chemotherapy initiation given the need for prolonged IV infusional 5-FU access. This will be arranged with general/vascular surgery promptly.

Neuropathy risk: The patient has pre-existing grade 1 bilateral sensory neuropathy from diabetic peripheral neuropathy. This is clinically important as oxaliplatin-induced cumulative peripheral neuropathy is anticipated and may be more problematic given this baseline. He will be monitored closely with low threshold for dose modification.

Blood pressure: Bevacizumab can worsen hypertension. Pre-existing hypertension is already managed with lisinopril. Blood pressure will be closely monitored at each visit; the antihypertensive regimen will be adjusted as needed.

Metformin: May continue metformin; no oxalate crystal formation risk anticipated at standard dosing. Glucose monitoring will be maintained, and we will watch for metformin-associated lactic acidosis with any major surgical procedure or IV contrast administration.

Patient counseling: An extensive discussion was held with Mr. Okafor and his wife regarding the goals of therapy. This is a palliative-intent treatment, as stage IV colorectal cancer is generally not curable with systemic chemotherapy alone. However, a meaningful proportion of patients with liver-limited metastatic colorectal cancer who achieve significant response may be candidates for hepatic-directed therapy, which in select patients can result in prolonged disease control and, rarely, long-term survival. This possibility was presented as a realistic but not guaranteed goal. The expected toxicities of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab were reviewed in detail, including peripheral neuropathy (particularly with his underlying diabetic neuropathy), fatigue, myelosuppression, mucositis, hypertension, potential wound healing issues with bevacizumab, and rare but serious risks including bowel perforation with bevacizumab (low risk given no peritoneal disease), arterial thromboembolic events, and VTE.

Mr. Okafor expressed understanding and consent to proceed. He emphasized his commitment to treatment and desire to remain as functional as possible in his professional role. He was encouraged to inform us of any side effects promptly.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. STAGE IV SIGMOID COLON ADENOCARCINOMA, KRAS G12D MUTANT, MSS, pT3 N2a M1a, SYNCHRONOUS BILOBAR HEPATIC METASTASES:
   - Discussed at multidisciplinary tumor board today with hepatobiliary surgery, colorectal surgery, and interventional radiology.
   - Plan: Initiate first-line palliative-intent systemic chemotherapy with modified FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab.
     - Oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV day 1 (will dose-reduce promptly for neuropathy given pre-existing grade 1 baseline)
     - Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1
     - 5-Fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1
     - 5-Fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours (via implanted port)
     - Bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV day 1
     - Cycle length: every 14 days
   - Doses based on BSA 2.01 m2.
   - Antiemetic prophylaxis: ondansetron 8 mg IV plus dexamethasone 10 mg IV prior to chemotherapy; prochlorperazine or ondansetron as needed at home for breakthrough nausea.
   - G-CSF (filgrastim/pegfilgrastim): Not routinely planned; will reassess if febrile neutropenia occurs.
   - Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis after cycle 6 (approximately 3 months).
   - CEA and CA 19-9 at each visit.
   - Tumor board to reassess hepatic-directed therapy eligibility after 6-8 cycles.
   - Anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab) is CONTRAINDICATED given KRAS G12D mutation -- documented.
   - Immune checkpoint inhibitors not indicated given MSS status -- documented.
   - KRAS G12D-targeted investigational agents: No standard-of-care options currently available; will monitor clinical trial landscape.

2. PORT PLACEMENT:
   - Referral placed for subcutaneous implanted venous access port placement (right chest wall preferred, or left per surgeon discretion).
   - Aspirin to be held approximately 7 days prior to port placement procedure.
   - Chemotherapy to begin after port confirmed functional.

3. PRE-EXISTING DIABETIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (GRADE 1):
   - Baseline neuropathy assessment documented.
   - Will monitor oxaliplatin-induced neurotoxicity at each cycle visit.
   - Dose modification algorithm: Grade 2 persistent neuropathy --> reduce oxaliplatin to 75 mg/m2; Grade 3 neuropathy --> hold/discontinue oxaliplatin per institutional protocol.
   - Neurology referral available PRN.
   - Continue metformin for diabetes management; hold for major surgery or IV contrast as appropriate.

4. HYPERTENSION:
   - Continue lisinopril 10 mg daily.
   - Bevacizumab initiation expected to raise blood pressure; will monitor BP at each chemotherapy visit.
   - Plan to adjust antihypertensive regimen as needed; may add amlodipine or increase lisinopril dose if bevacizumab-related hypertension develops.

5. HYPERLIPIDEMIA:
   - Continue atorvastatin 40 mg nightly. No change at this time.

6. MILD NORMOCYTIC ANEMIA (Hgb 11.4 g/dL):
   - Likely anemia of chronic disease/malignancy.
   - Will recheck CBC with each cycle.
   - Transfusion not indicated at this hemoglobin level in the setting of ECOG PS 1 and no cardiopulmonary symptoms.
   - Iron studies to be obtained to rule out concurrent iron deficiency.

7. SOCIAL AND PSYCHOLOGICAL SUPPORT:
   - Patient and family counseled extensively today regarding diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment plan.
   - Referral to oncology social work placed.
   - Referral to oncology nutritionist/dietitian placed.
   - Patient information materials provided including written summary of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab and expected toxicities.
   - Encouraged to call oncology triage line for any concerning symptoms (fever >38.0 C, inability to tolerate oral intake, bleeding, severe diarrhea, chest pain, shortness of breath).

8. FOLLOW-UP:
   - Port placement to be scheduled within 1-2 weeks.
   - First cycle of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab to begin after port placement.
   - Return visit for cycle 1 day 1 chemotherapy; mid-cycle toxicity check by telephone at day 5-7.
   - Routine oncology follow-up every 2 weeks (each infusion visit).
   - Formal restaging CT after cycle 6.

Time spent in counseling and coordination with patient and family: approximately 60 minutes.

All questions answered. Plan reviewed and agreed upon by patient and spouse. Patient consents to proceed with treatment.

---
Electronically signed by Attending Medical Oncologist
